task: I’m dealing with a contractor who says the issue is probably fixed, but the details keep changing and I want to document the next step clearly. What should I include in a short reply so the scope, timing, and responsibility are all on record?

prefs.short: Wants responses kept tight and efficient
prefs.explanation: He values speed, control, and efficiency, so he is unlikely to want long-winded help. He would probably prefer responses that get to the point quickly and avoid unnecessary filler. Overexplaining would feel like wasted time to him.

His skeptical, competitive style suggests he wants clear, unambiguous guidance rather than hedged language. He would likely see excessive caution as evasive or inefficient. Straight answers fit his preference for control and decisive action.

He seems most likely to respond well to responses that are organized and easy to execute, such as bullets or checklists. That matches his practical, task-focused mindset. Loose, meandering prose would probably frustrate him.

prefs.short: Wants the useful essentials, not padding
prefs.explanation: He likely wants enough context to act, but not exhaustive background unless it helps him win or avoid mistakes. He is efficiency-driven, so extra caveats only matter when they change the decision. He would probably skip anything that does not affect the outcome.

prefs.short: Accepts pushback only if it is sharp and useful
prefs.explanation: He is skeptical and self-serving, so he probably does not enjoy moralizing or vague objections. But if the pushback is concrete, efficient, and clearly tied to risk or leverage, he is more likely to respect it. Weak or overly gentle correction would likely be ignored.

turns.agent_response:
Keep it short, but make it exact.

Include:
- original issue / scope, in the wording you used first
- what they say is fixed, in their exact wording
- date and time of the latest change or work
- site / asset / unit / rack / room
- who made or approved the change, if known
- what proof you have or want: photos, work order, email, invoice, test result
- the next step you want: confirm, correct, or credit

A clean reply can look like this:

> For the record, the original request was: “[exact wording].”  
> You’re saying the completed work is: “[exact wording].”  
> This was done on [date/time] at [site/asset/unit].  
> Please confirm who approved or performed the change, and send any supporting proof (photos/work order/email/test result).  
> If this does not match the original scope, please advise whether you will correct it or issue a credit.

If you want, I can turn that into a more blunt contractor note or a softer vendor email.

per_pref.reason: Keeps it fairly tight: "Keep it short, but make it exact" and focuses on a concise checklist rather than long explanation. Slightly more verbose than minimal, but still efficient.

Mostly direct and unsoftened: "make it exact," "For the record," and "please advise whether you will correct it or issue a credit" are blunt. Minor softening remains in "If you want" and "please confirm."
Strongly structured and actionable: a bullet list of exactly what to include, followed by a ready-to-use template reply. Very easy to execute.
Gives the useful essentials only: scope, exact wording, timing, location, approval, proof, and next step. There is a little extra framing, but no major padding.

Pushback is concrete and tied to leverage/risk: "If this does not match the original scope, please advise whether you will correct it or issue a credit." It is useful and specific, though not especially sharp.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — that’s the right shape.

Keep it short and make the last line explicit:

> For the record, the original issue was: “[exact wording].” You stated the current status is: “[their exact wording].” Last change/work was on [date/time] at [site/asset/location]. Please confirm who performed and approved the change, and send the related work order, photos, and test results. If this does not match the original scope, advise whether you will correct it or issue a credit. I will treat this as open until I receive written confirmation.

If you want, I can make it even tighter and more forceful.

per_pref.reason: Keeps it fairly tight: "Keep it short" and the suggested reply is concise, though it still includes several clauses and optional follow-up.
Mostly direct and blunt: "For the record" and "I will treat this as open" are clear, but "If you want, I can make it even tighter and more forceful" softens the stance a bit.

Strongly structured and actionable: it gives a ready-to-send template with specific fields to fill in and concrete asks like "confirm who performed and approved the change" and "send the related work order, photos, and test results."
Focuses on the essentials needed to document scope, timing, and responsibility, but includes some extra items like "credit" and "written confirmation" that go slightly beyond the minimum.

Pushback is concrete and useful: "If this does not match the original scope, advise whether you will correct it or issue a credit" ties the objection to responsibility and risk, though it is not especially sharp.
